segments.s737-g41: "چو" is the temporal conjunction 'when'; "خسرو" is the subject (the name Khosrow). "بپردخت" is an older/poetic past form meaning 'he turned/devoted himself' (compare پرداخت), so the verb is past 3rd‑singular. "چندی" is an adverb 'for a while', and "به مهر" is a prepositional phrase 'to/for love' meaning 'to love' or 'to his love' — together: 'when Khosrow for a while devoted himself to love.' Word order is conjunction + subject + verb + duration adverb + prepositional complement.
segments.s737-g42: "شب و روز" ('night and day') is an adverbial phrase meaning 'constantly'. "گریان" is a participial/adjectival form 'weeping' used predicatively. "بدی" here is an archaic/poetic past copula (compare بود/بودی) meaning 'was' (not the noun 'evil'), so it links the participle to the subject. "خوب‌چهر" ('fair‑faced') is the subject; in poetry the subject often follows the verb phrase, so the whole clause reads 'the fair‑faced one was weeping night and day.'
segments.s737-g25: 'چنین گفت' is a fixed phrase 'thus said' (چنین = 'thus/so' before the verb گفت = 'said'). The speaker identification that follows, 'داننده دهقان پیر', is a noun phrase with postpositive modifiers: داننده 'knowing/learned' qualifies the noun phrase 'دهقان پیر' (an old peasant). Word order is typical poetic Persian: adverbial 'چنین' + verb 'گفت' + the speaker description.
segments.s737-g26: که introduces a reported content clause ('that'). The clause has the copula construction 'دانش بود ... دستگیر' in an older/poetic order: 'دانش' (subject) + 'بود' (was) + 'مرد را دستگیر' (lit. 'for the man, a helper'). 'مرد را' is the definite/affected phrase marked by را and placed before the predicative noun 'دستگیر'. Here 'دستگیر' is predicative/adjectival meaning 'helping/a succor', not 'seized'.
segments.s737-g27: The phrase is impersonal/general: 'غم و شادمانی' (sorrow and joy) is the theme, followed by the modal 'بباید' ('one must' / obligation). The verb 'کشید' here is poetic for 'to bear/endure' (an old form of کشیدن used with بباید to express necessity). Word order places the objects before the modal+verb ('... بباید کشید').
segments.s737-g28: 'ز' is the poetic preposition 'from' (equivalent to modern از). 'هر شور و تلخی' = 'every passion and bitterness' is the source/complement of the verb. Again 'بباید' marks obligation and 'چشید' ('to taste/experience') is used figuratively: 'one must experience/taste'. Word order is modal + verbal stem 'بباید چشید'.
segments.s737-g29: 'جوانان' is the vocative/topic 'youths' (plural). The following adjectives/epithets 'داننده و باگهر' come after the noun (a common Persian order): داننده 'learned/knowing' and باگهر literally 'with jewels' — metaphorically 'distinguished/precious'. The sequence addresses the young people with praise before giving counsel.
segments.s737-g30: 'نگیرند' is a negative present/subjunctive/optative form meaning 'they should not take/assume' (the ن- prefix here forms the negative in classical usage; the verb is 3rd-person plural). 'بی آزمایش' = 'without test/trial' modifies the following noun 'هنر' which is the object meaning 'skill/art'. The whole clause is an admonition: do not claim skill without having been tested.
segments.s737-g31: چو here means 'when' introducing a temporal clause. 'پرویز' is the proper name. 'ناباک بود و جوان' = 'was virtuous/pure and young' — the copula بود governs both predicates (ناباک and جوان) even though it appears only once. The structure is classical/poetic but maps to 'When Parviz was virtuous and young...'.
segments.s737-g32: This coordinate clause continues the description: 'پدر زنده' = 'the father (was) alive' (زنده = alive), 'و پور چون پهلوان' = 'and the son (was) like a champion' — چون 'like/as' compares پور (son) to a پهلوان (hero). The copula 'was' is omitted but understood from the prior clause.
segments.s737-g9: اگر introduces a conditional clause ('if'). بازجویی is a noun 'inspection/examination' functioning as the nominal content of the condition. درو is the archaic contraction در او ('in it' / 'in that'), a locative clitic pointing back to the book/text; بیت بد is a noun + following adjective (classical Persian places the adjective after the noun) meaning 'bad couplets/verses.'
segments.s737-g10: همانا is an emphatic adverb 'verily/truly.' که here introduces the explanatory/assertive clause ('that...'). کم is the predicate 'few/insufficient.' باشد is the classical subjunctive/present copula used here to assert existence/quantity ('is/are'). از پانصد literally 'from five hundred' = 'less than five hundred.'
segments.s737-g11: چنین is a demonstrative/adverb 'such' or 'so.' شهریاری here names the royal quality (an abstract or attributive use meaning 'kingly/sovereign' or 'sovereignty'); و links to بخشنده‌ای, an indefinite nominal form with the -ای suffix meaning 'a generous one.' Word order places the quality words together as a nominal description.
segments.s737-g12: به گیتی is a locative phrase 'in the world' (classical Persian often uses به + noun for location/setting). ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/of' used to mean 'among' or 'of the class of.' شاهان is the plural 'kings,' and درخشنده‌ای is a nominal/participial adjective 'a shining/radiant one,' again with the indefinite -ای; the whole phrase says he is radiant among the world's kings.
segments.s737-g13: نکرد is the simple past negative of کردن ('did not do') used here with the noun نگاه to mean 'did not look' or 'did not cast a glance.' اندرین is a fused prepositional/demonstrative 'in these' (an older form of در این), and داستانها is the plural 'tales/stories.' The verb (نکرد) precedes the object phrase, a common order in epic/classical lines.
segments.s737-g14: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of.' بدگوی denotes those who speak ill or slander ('the slanderer' or 'ill-speakers'); و links the two causes. بخت بد is the collocation 'bad fortune' (noun + adjective, adjective after noun). آمد is the simple past 'came/happened' and گناه is the result 'sin/fault/blame'—so 'blame/sin befell (came) from slanderers and bad luck.'
segments.s737-g15: حسد (a noun) + کرد (the past 'did') forms a light verbal construction meaning 'envied.' بدگوی is the agent/subject 'the slanderer' and appears after the verb (permissible inversion in poetic/classical Persian). در کار من is a prepositional phrase 'in my affairs' that marks the domain of the envy.
segments.s737-g16: تبه here means 'ruin/mischief' and شد is the past 'became' ('became ruined'). بر شاه is a prepositional phrase 'before/in the presence of/with respect to the king' (classical use of بر), and بازار من 'my market/business' is the possessed noun phrase that suffered the misfortune. Word order places the locative/beneficiary phrase immediately after شد for emphasis.
segments.s737-g1: کهن گشته (perfect participle + auxiliary sense) precedes the noun phrase for poetic emphasis: 'has become old / grown ancient.' این نامه باستان is the noun phrase 'this ancient record/book' (این = demonstrative before the head noun). Word order is inverted for rhythm: participle + noun rather than the more prosaic 'این نامه کهن گشته.'
segments.s737-g2: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/through/because of' linking cause to the preceding clause. گفتار و کردار is the linked coordinate noun phrase 'speech and deed(s)' with و connecting the two. آن راستان (آن + راستان) points back to people mentioned earlier: آن = 'those / the' and راستان is the substantive 'the righteous ones' (plural) serving as the agent/source of the 'from'.
segments.s737-g3: همی is an older adverb marking continuous or habitual action ('ever / continually / now'), placed before the verb for emphasis. نوکنم is first‑person present/subjunctive/indicative in classical style meaning 'I renew / make new again.' گفته‌ها is the plural object 'the sayings / statements.' زین here is the fused prepositional phrase 'ز این' = 'from this' (classical spelling); the lemma senses given don't include this meaning so the semantic code is unknown.
segments.s737-g4: ز is again the preposition 'from.' گفتار بیدار is a genitive/noun + adjective sequence 'the utterance/word of the wakeful (bیدار),' where بیدار qualifies گفتار (poetic allowance to place the adjective after the noun). مرد کهن is a noun + adjective 'an aged/ancient man.' The whole segment supplies the source of the sayings named in the previous segment.
segments.s737-g5: بود is the past form 'there was / there were' introducing existence. بیت here means 'poetic couplet(s)' (the subject). شش بار reads as a multiplicative phrase 'six times'; بار functions as 'times/occasions.' بیور is an obscure word/name in the manuscript here (its precise numeric or onomastic role is unclear), so it is left untranslated; it appears in the quantifying cluster that follows بیت.
segments.s737-g6: سخنهای is the plural possessive/attributive form 'sayings / speeches / utterances' modifying the adjectives that follow. شایسته is an adjective 'worthy / appropriate,' and غمگسار is an adjective/noun meaning 'sorrow-sharing / consoling' applied to the speech; together they qualify the بیت(s) just mentioned.
segments.s737-g7: نبیند is a classical negative perfect/present form meaning 'has not seen / does not see' (in context here, 'no one has seen'). کسی is used with the negated verb to yield 'no one' (negated indefinite). نامه پارسی is the noun phrase 'a Persian book/treatise' with پارسی qualifying نامه.
segments.s737-g8: نوشته is participial/adjectival 'written.' به + ابیات marks the medium/measure 'in (so many) verses' (classic Persian uses به before a numeric measure). صدبار here literally reads 'a hundred times' (and in the manuscript the following 'سی' completes the number to yield 'صد بار سی' = 100×30 = 3000), so the whole phrase gives the length of the work in verses.
segments.s737-g17: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. سالار شاه is a compounded title phrase (سالار 'commander/lord' + شاه 'king') serving as the clause subject; the noun phrase این سخنهای نغز ('these fine words') is placed after the subject as the direct object that the following verb will govern. Word order is typical older Persian: conjunction + subject + object.
segments.s737-g18: بخواند and ببیند are two coordinated 3rd‑person verbs describing what the subject does and then perceives; the ب‑ prefix here is the classical narrative/optative prefix used in epic style. به پاکیزه نغز is an adverbial/qualifying phrase of manner ('in a pure, delightful way') modifying how he 'saw' or judged the words.
segments.s737-g19: ز introduces a source/cause ('from' or 'with'); گنجش من is an older genitive construction meaning 'my treasure' (گنجِ من). ایدر ('here') is an adverbial locative; شوم is an optative/volitive verb form 'may I become', and شادمان is the predicative adjective 'happy'. The whole clause is a wish: 'May I be happy here with my treasure.'
segments.s737-g20: کزو = 'from/of him' (or 'from which') begins a short optative clause. دور بادا is a fixed order with بادا as the optative particle 'may it be far'; بد بدگمان is the nominal phrase 'ill/evil suspicion' (بد qualifying بدگمان). Together the clause means 'may ill suspicion be far from him.'
segments.s737-g21: وزان پس is a connective phrase ('and from that, then/afterwards') introducing what follows. کند is the 3rd‑person present/past narrative 'he makes/does' and with یاد بر شهریار ('a reminder to the king') functions as 'he reminded the king' (یاد here used as 'reminder'). بر marks the indirect object/direction 'to' (toward the sovereign).
segments.s737-g22: مگر introduces a negative purpose/condition here ('lest' / 'unless'). تخم رنج من is a possessive noun phrase 'the seed of my suffering' (من as genitive 'of me'). آید ببار is a two‑word verbal expression: آید 'may come' and ببار (literally 'pour' or 'fall') used idiomatically to mean 'come to fruition/come to pass' — together conveying 'should come to bear.'
segments.s737-g23: که introduces a wish/exclamation clause ('that'/'may it be that'). جاوید باد is a conventional optative formula ('may ... be eternal') in which باد is the optative particle. افسر و تخت اوی lists two possessions ('diadem/helmet and throne') followed by اوی, an archaic possessive form meaning 'his', so the phrase reads 'may his diadem and throne be eternal.'
segments.s737-g24: ز here is the poetic comparative preposition 'than/from' used to set up a comparison. خورشید تابنده‌تر is 'the sun, more shining' (تابنده‌تر is the comparative form 'more radiant/shining'), and بخت اوی is 'his fortune'. The clause literally says 'his fortune more shining than the sun,' i.e. 'may his fortune be more radiant than the shining sun.'
segments.s737-g33: ورا is an object pronoun ('to him/her/it') placed before the rest of the phrase. در زمین is a prepositional phrase ('in/on the earth') giving location. دوست شیرین is a noun phrase in apposition ('the sweet beloved, Shirin'); بدی here is a poetic/archaic verbal form (see note) serving as the main verb meaning roughly 'gave/placed' though that exact verbal sense is not listed among the provided noun senses.
segments.s737-g34: برو is the verb form literally 'go' but in classical usage with بر can mean 'set/bring before' (i.e. 'placed before'). بر is the preposition 'upon/to'. چو روشن جهان‌بین is a descriptive simile ('like the bright/clear-sighted, world-seeing one'). The final بدی is the same archaic/poetic verbal form as in the previous segment (used here as the main past action); that verbal sense is not represented in the provided noun senses.
segments.s737-g35: پسندش is a noun 'his liking/what he prefers' with the enclitic -ش ('his'). نبودی is the negative past copula ('was not'); here it means 'there was not' or 'he had not'. جزو در جهان is an idiomatic noun phrase meaning 'a peer/part in the world' — together the clause says 'there was no equal in the world'.
segments.s737-g36: ز (poetic for 'از') introduces source/group: 'from/among'. خوبان here means 'the beautiful/the beloved ones' (i.e. the handsome/beautiful people). و is the coordinating 'and'. از is the plain 'from/among'. دختران is 'maidens/daughters'. مهان in classical poetry often denotes notable/noble persons; the provided senses list 'guest/visitors' does not match that usage exactly (see senses mapping).
segments.s737-g37: ز is the poetic preposition 'from' (here 'from Shirin'). شیرین is the proper name Shirin. جدا بود is the past-state predicate 'was apart/separated'. یک روزگار means 'for a time' or 'one period'.
segments.s737-g38: بدان گه is a classical locution meaning 'at that time' (built from به/ب + آن + گه; the middle element as given in the word list is ambiguous with its listed senses). که introduces a subordinate clause 'when/that'. بد here means 'ill/poorly' describing حال/condition. در جهان شهریار = 'the king in the world' (i.e. 'the king of the realm'); the whole clause reads 'at that time when the king was ill in the world.'
segments.s737-g39: بگرد جهان در is an inverted classical word order meaning 'around the world' or 'throughout the world' (بگرد here is historically related to turning/going about and functions adverbially; the imperative/sense listed in the word data doesn't reflect this idiomatic adverbial use). بی‌آرام بود is the predicate 'was unrestful/restless.'
segments.s737-g40: که is the conjunction 'for/that' introducing the reason. کارش is 'his work/affairs' (کار + enclitic ش). همه رزم means 'all (of it) was battle' — همه modifies the noun رزم 'war/battle'. بهرام is a proper name (here the king Bahram or the poetic reference to Mars) specifying whose wars or the martial aspect; بود is the past copula 'was.'

sentences.s737-s21: When Khosrow for a while gave himself to love, the fair‑faced one wept night and day.
sentences.s737-s13: Thus said the knowing old peasant: that knowledge was man's helper.
sentences.s737-s14: One must endure sorrow and joy; from every passion and bitterness one must taste.
sentences.s737-s15: O learned and jewel-bedecked youths, do not assume skill without trial.
sentences.s737-s16: When Parviz was pure and young, the father was alive and the son was like a champion.
sentences.s737-s5: If one inspects it, the bad couplets in it are, truly, fewer than five hundred.
sentences.s737-s6: Such a sovereign and generous one, resplendent among the kings of the world.
sentences.s737-s7: He did not cast a glance on these tales; from slanderers and ill fortune came blame.
sentences.s737-s8: The slanderer envied my affairs, and my market/business was ruined before the king.
sentences.s737-s1: This ancient record has grown old because of the words and deeds of those righteous ones.
sentences.s737-s2: I renew these sayings from this speech — from the utterance of a wakeful, ancient man.
sentences.s737-s3: There were couplets — (six ×) Biur thousand — worthy and consoling sayings.
sentences.s737-s4: No one has seen a Persian book written in three thousand verses.
sentences.s737-s9: When the king's commander read these fine words, he perceived them to be pure and delightful.
sentences.s737-s10: May I be happy here with my treasure; may ill suspicion be far from him.
sentences.s737-s11: And after that he reminded the sovereign, lest the seed of my suffering should come to bear.
sentences.s737-s12: May his diadem and throne be eternal, and may his fortune be more radiant than the shining sun.
sentences.s737-s17: You gave him, on earth, Shirin the sweet beloved; you placed him before the bright, world-seeing one.
sentences.s737-s18: There was no one he liked in the world among the beauties and among the noble maidens.
sentences.s737-s19: For a time he was apart from Shirin — at that moment when the king was ill in the world.
sentences.s737-s20: All over the world there was unrest, for all his affairs were war (Bahrām).
